meta.title: CSP00 Personal Knowledge Graph
meta.description: A starter knowledge graph designed for both human reading and agent retrieval.

nodes.title: Semantic HTML
nodes.summary: Use meaningful HTML elements to improve structure, accessibility, and machine understanding.
nodes.learningObjective: Be able to choose appropriate HTML elements for content sections, forms, and media.
commonErrors: Using div for headings, sections, and navigation
verificationSteps: Check whether headings and sections form a clear document outline
Check whether form controls have labels

nodes.title: CSS Flexbox
nodes.summary: A one-dimensional layout system for alignment, spacing, and wrapping.
nodes.learningObjective: Build responsive rows and galleries with flex containers and flex items.
commonErrors: Setting flex properties on the wrong element
verificationSteps: Resize the viewport and confirm the layout wraps correctly
Check horizontal and vertical alignment independently

nodes.title: CSS Animation
nodes.summary: Animate UI elements and CSS art using keyframes, transforms, and timing functions.
nodes.learningObjective: Create continuous and state-based animations for decorative or instructional interfaces.
commonErrors: Animating layout properties instead of transform when possible
verificationSteps: Confirm the motion is smooth and loops as expected
Verify the animation still makes sense when reduced motion is preferred

nodes.title: What Is AI?
nodes.summary: AI can be framed along two axes (human vs. rational, thought vs. behavior), yielding four classic approaches.
nodes.learningObjective: Understand the four foundational perspectives used to define AI.
commonErrors: Equating AI with machine learning only
Mixing human-like behavior with rational optimality
verificationSteps: Explain the two axes and produce four combinations
Distinguish acting rationally from thinking humanly

nodes.title: Turing Test Approach
nodes.summary: A machine is judged intelligent if an interrogator cannot distinguish it from a human through text interaction.
nodes.learningObjective: Describe capabilities required by the Turing test and total Turing test.
commonErrors: Treating Turing test as the only definition of intelligence
Ignoring perception/robotics in total Turing test
verificationSteps: List NLP, representation, reasoning, learning
Explain why total Turing test adds vision and robotics

nodes.title: Rational Agent
nodes.summary: A rational agent selects actions to achieve the best outcome or best expected outcome under uncertainty.
nodes.learningObjective: Explain why the rational-agent model became the dominant AI framing.
commonErrors: Assuming rational action always requires explicit logical inference
Confusing perfect rationality with practical bounded computation
verificationSteps: Compare laws-of-thought vs rational-agent approaches
Give an example where reflex is more rational than long deliberation

nodes.title: Value Alignment Problem
nodes.summary: Machine objectives must align with human preferences; otherwise capable systems can optimize for unintended behavior.
nodes.learningObjective: Understand why fixed objectives are often inadequate in real-world AI deployment.
commonErrors: Believing objective misspecification is harmless at high capability
Assuming simulator fixes transfer safely to real-world deployment
verificationSteps: Use self-driving tradeoffs to explain alignment ambiguity
Explain why beneficial AI must remain uncertain about human objectives

nodes.title: Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach (4th Edition)
nodes.summary: Core textbook source node for AI foundational concepts.
nodes.learningObjective: Track canonical textbook provenance for AI definitions.
verificationSteps: Cross-check chapter and section locators.

nodes.title: AIMA 4e Chapter 1 Section 1.1 What Is AI?
nodes.summary: Defines AI through four approaches and introduces rational-agent framing.
nodes.learningObjective: Provide fine-grained textual evidence for AI foundational nodes.
verificationSteps: Validate locator Chapter 1, Section 1.1.

nodes.title: AIMA 4e 1.1.1 Acting Humanly: Turing Test
nodes.summary: Introduces Turing test and capability requirements including NLP, reasoning and learning.
nodes.learningObjective: Anchor Turing-test knowledge to explicit textbook passage.
verificationSteps: Validate locator Chapter 1, 1.1.1.

nodes.title: AIMA 4e 1.1.4 Acting Rationally: Rational Agent
nodes.summary: Defines rational agent and explains why this approach became dominant.
nodes.learningObjective: Ground rational-agent node in canonical source.
verificationSteps: Validate locator Chapter 1, 1.1.4.

nodes.title: AIMA 4e 1.1.5 Beneficial Machines
nodes.summary: Introduces value alignment and argues for provably beneficial AI systems.
nodes.learningObjective: Ground alignment and beneficial-machine concepts in primary source.
verificationSteps: Validate locator Chapter 1, 1.1.5.

nodes.title: Cat Photo Gallery
nodes.summary: A gallery project used to practice flexbox, image sizing, and wrapping behavior.
nodes.learningObjective: Connect layout knowledge to a concrete visual gallery page.
commonErrors: Images stretching because width and height are not constrained
Items not wrapping on small screens
verificationSteps: Open the page and resize the viewport
Check that gallery items keep consistent proportions

nodes.title: Ferris Wheel
nodes.summary: A CSS animation project that demonstrates rotation, timing, and transform-based motion.
nodes.learningObjective: Translate animation principles into a playful, inspectable artifact.
commonErrors: Incorrect absolute positioning for spokes and cabins
verificationSteps: Ensure the wheel rotates continuously
Check whether cabins maintain a readable orientation

edges.reason: Semantic structure still matters even in simple gallery pages.
The project uses flexbox for image arrangement and wrapping.
The project is built around keyframes and transform animation.
Both are core CSS skills often combined in UI work.
A clear document structure should come before layout refinement.
The project can be used as evidence that flexbox concepts were practiced.
The project demonstrates working knowledge of animation timing and transforms.
Section 1.1 introduces AI through four approach combinations.
AIMA serves as the canonical textbook context.
Subsection 1.1.1 defines the Turing test and required capabilities.
The subsection is part of the four-approach framing in 1.1.
Subsection 1.1.4 explicitly defines rational agent and expected outcome criterion.
General framing of rationality in Section 1.1 supports interpretation.
Subsection 1.1.5 introduces value alignment and beneficial-machine framing.
Examples such as self-driving tradeoffs are cited from this subsection.
Alignment extends the standard rational-agent objective formulation.
